speaker: interviewee
text: Really was at a crossroads at that point, because I realized that we had some-- two young men staying with my parents. With my parents, we always felt the lucky ones because I had both my parents who survived and joined a youth group, German, Austrian, and Czech youth groups in London when I went back to England. And most of them were refugees. And we had a club that was always open, and so on. And really, the majority of the children there-- we were young teenagers at that time-- had lost, or didn't know, the whereabouts of their parents. And my father and mother were basically the at home. We would-- my brother was also, then, had graduated and came to London. And it was open house at my parents' house. We would bring in and they would counsel. And we had an engagement party for one couple. There was a tremendous support system in my house of other friends and peers who had no relatives, who came on a Kindertransporter, or on their own, or whatever, under what circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a very large refugee nucleus. But always, I consider myself the very lucky one. Then the displaced persons came, and they were not welcomed as much. They had-- they were much more--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] --disturbed. They had much more to learn about assimilating and, and being-- angelicized is the wrong word. I was-- I was-- I was pretty British by then. But I knew it would be too difficult. And I always felt Israel is the place where they should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rather than in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we now at this point [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in college still. It was '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you, during, during your time in England during the war, were you or and family were aware of anything going on in the rest of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Very little. Now, don't forget, I was in a totally English, non-Jewish, atmosphere away. Most of the-- from '39 on to '41, end of '41, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had all my energy invested in learning and adapting to the different ways, and the language, and the culture, and so on. I was not really very aware.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was looked down on. I loved classical music. I liked to read. But my foster parents lower-middle class, and I was considered to be a snob. So I sort of squelched all that. I became one of them. And I really wasn't wise what was going on at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, so the--

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, I think, were. Certainly they made inquiries. My father worked with the refugee committees in getting more foster homes, and supervising the refugee children, and heard more. But I'm not sure that-- I remember getting the news that my grandmother and my aunt had been killed, and seeing the letter, the notification from the Red Cross, and my father trying to snatch that letter away from me. You see, parents try to protect their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did any news of any of the camp-- what was happening in the concentration--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure that my father and mother did, but only post-war. Then we heard it firsthand from the DP young people who stayed with us. And as I said, we knew then what had happened. But very, very-- I didn't get involved in that. I got involved with the actual hand-on teaching of English, and joined the joint distribution committee, and became a social worker, And worked with neglected children. And then when I went with the DPs, I went via France to Israel with a group. Because I felt-- I emigrated to Israel with a group of DPs, in order to help them settle. I always felt very, very lucky, because nothing really happened to me. When I heard the stories of how they lived in the camps, and how they suffered. I was the lucky one. Nothing ever happened to me. I were taken away from home, yes, but there was foods and there was shelter. And there were people, even though we didn't like them very much and they didn't like us-- but we had the basic needs were fulfilled.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I really never felt anything happened to me, until I worked with the DPs. And then I realized what went on in Europe, from their stories. And it was mind boggling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had what was going on in Europe. And then, of course, I heard about grandmother, and this uncle, and that uncle. And then gradually after the war, we heard that information. Very little was talked about. Your duty is to become a staunch citizen, and to learn, and to build up a new future. That was the attitude. And as I said, I went to Israel. And went into kibbutz-- it's a long story-- and worked with the DPs. To some extent, they were integrated into one of the youth groups in the-- in the-- in the [? kibbutz ?] movement. I lived in Israel for a year, met my husband, my first husband. And he-- his family came from Berlin, went to South America. And, when I met him, he already had a visa to go to Brazil to be reunited with his parents in South America.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, anyway, I went to South America eventually to follow him. And worked with the JDC, with the Joint Distribution Committee, with the DPs in Brazil.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been in Israel and they couldn't settle. And then they went-- they tried different countries, and they were-- joint sent them to Brazil. Either because they had somebody else there-- I don't know what made them come. But my function was to integrate the DPs, as best as possible, into the Brazilian community. That was my job with JDC. And then three-- we were there for three years. My son was born in Brazil. We-- my husband wanted to do some more studying, and we got a job in Germany. He became the youth director in Berlin for the Jewish community, and for the [SPEAKING GERMAN] for the Jewish community for returnees. And I worked for JDC doing some research, and that was a fascinating thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war-- that was in the '50s, early '50s. No. Wait a minute, no. '51 I went to Israel, there we lived to '55. My son was born-- '57. '57 to '59, we were back in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel to go back to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Awful. Awful. Now we were VIPs. We were A, Jewish-- we were-- he was working for the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, for the German government, as well as for the German-Jewish community, as well as for the, the umbrella agency, which was the Jewish agency in Frankfurt. And he was the youth director. And he was-- had a place in-- he was a VIP. He mixed very much with the elite of the German society, the Social Democrats. But we were placed in a bombed out home-- you know, it was after the war. Berlin was bombed very badly-- in a bombed out apartment, in a very-- in a proletarian kind of neighborhood with a young baby. And we evicted, as it were, one of the ladies who lived there for 40 years because she was a widow, and she wasn't allowed to have a two-bedroom apartment. We had three people. You know, it was this sort of thing. And those people were nasty. As my son was just beginning to walk, you know, they would take the broomstick and knock. And they wouldn't say good morning. It was a horrible experience. But then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were actually living among people who had--

speaker: interviewee
text: None of them were Nazis, you know. And my son was two-years old. And we were playing in the sandbox-- there was a park opposite-- and I invited them for a birthday party. And they said they would come. Typical German mothers with their little ones. And I said you'll come at, I don't know, two o'clock or whatever, four o'clock. And I had balloons, and I told my son, if you go to sleep, you know, we'll have a party. Nobody came. I met them again in the street and-- like that. It was the same darn thing again. Don't talk to the Jews. It felt exactly the same. It was a horrible time, until I started working for the Jewish community, I got-- I was a kindergarten, and I did the curriculum for the nursery school. They were returnees, also DPs. They had lost their original spouses, and then met another DP and formed a new family. And had old parents with young children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had-- I became a social worker, also a psychiatric social worker, in England. I did the counseling of the curriculum for some of those. And did some things for JDC.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were people in Berlin who, at the end, before they were taken away to concentration camps, had babies, which they left with non-Jewish, usually maids. And said to take care of my baby. Now, these babies were raised by these non-Jews, and the parents perished. In '57 when I was there, an uncle-- I remember one particularly. Relatives from all over the world said my sister, Lanie Schwartz, or whatever the name was, had a baby on that and that date at that-- so on-- and has perished. We want that baby. Well, obviously, at that time, it wasn't a baby any longer. And it was my duty to do the research, to find that baby that was born then and then, and I did all the official investigation, and found 15 children, most of whom didn't know that they were not-- that they were Jewish. Also the foster parents knew and didn't tell them. There were a whole variation. I remember one, and I'd like to tell you about that one-- [? Yoden. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- he was still in school, and I arranged to meet the foster mother. In the school, I told the teachers what it was all about. Met the-- and she said, oh I'm so relieved. I've never told [? Yoden ?] what his real story is. But I've always felt badly about it. Would you come to the house, and would you tell him with me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And, of course, I had an inquiry from Johannesburg in South Africa from an uncle that he wanted this boy. And he was the only surviving uncle. I told [? Yoden ?] about it. And I saw this kid-- he was lighting up. His face said, I always thought I didn't fit in here. I always knew I was different. And I remember asking you, and you not answering me. And I never found out why you were also tone deaf, and I was loving music so. It turned out that uncle from South Africa came to visit [? Yoden ?] and paid for violin lessons. What happened, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were several of these [INAUDIBLE]. Most of them, I don't think anybody who had relatives outside of Germany went to be reunited. They all felt comfortable with their foster parents and stayed, to the best of my knowledge anyway. I haven't kept up contact.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was an interesting thing in, in Berlin. We stayed there for three years. Horrible. And then we came to America in 1960.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to tell you something what I did in America, because in retrospect, now that I've been asked to do this video, things sort of fall into place from a non-orthodox kind of a background-- not too Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my ancestors never denied their Judaism. And if it came to it, you know, they would stand up and say that they were Jews. But they were also good Germans, culturally, intellectually. They had been there since 1350, as far as I can trace back. And thinking about this interview, my thinking was, gee, this background-- I'm still pretty Germanic. I'm punctual, and I am still sometimes organized. And I like things to be the right way, to be efficient, and this sort of thing. And my mother's background with the Zionists must have made some influence in me. I was a teacher and a social worker. And why I'm saying this is, when I came to America, I looked for houses. We looked for houses. And I wanted an integrated community to live in. Not all Jewish, but I needed to be somewhere where there was a Jewish synagogue and a community. I just had that need. It was very interesting. I think it's partly because I was in Israel also. Anyway, in America, we only arrived in 1960. I worked in a nursery school in a synagogue. I had my own nursery school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In West Chester, Pennsylvania. And really find that I've become very Jewishly oriented. And am now working, since 1989, I suddenly found myself a survivor, which I never considered myself to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: I considered myself to be, yes, different, and an outsider wherever I was-- a bloody foreigner, as the English would say. But I never real-- it never really bothered me that much until-- in October, '88. I had a call and said, are you Eva Rosenbaum? Were you on the Kindertransport? You know, an interrogative kind of a voice. And I was back when the Nazis wanted to come in to look for my father. That voice, that authoritative voice, triggered something in me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, to cut a long story short, there was a Kindertransport reunion in 1989. And I had been-- I knew that at Gratz College there was an oral history archive of survivors. And Nora Levin was-- had this archive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I asked her, how many kinder stories there were in those archives? Most of them were from concentration camps. And the word survivor meant that you were in a concentration camp. I never considered myself a survivor. But as I was going to this reunion in London of the Kindertransport, I asked how many kinder stories do you have? And she said, none. So under the auspices of Gratz College, I went to Israel, I went to England, and I became an interviewer. And I have 20 odd tapes of kinder in the archives.

speaker: interviewee
text: And became, all of a sudden, aware that anybody can work with psychiatric patients. Anybody can teach children and be a social worker. I worked with the county for some years with-- as a caseworker with psychiatric patients. But I decided to interview kinder, and to have empathy with the survivors. Not everybody can do. So I quit my job. I retired-- took early retirement. And I have been working, basically, in this area for interviewing survivors, and helping, and counseling. It's the best of my ability to do that. And have a support group-- we have a Kindertransport Association in New York. And there are splinter groups all over the country. And a colleague, another kinder and I-- this very funny, who lives around the corner, and we didn't know until the conference-- have been speaking on the Holocaust, as we are both teachers, I feel very strongly that it is important for us to tell the stories. It is important to-- for the future generations and our children, our own children. I have two, and we have a-- an obligation to tell the younger generation of, of their roots and our happenings. So this is what I have been doing. But you can only talk so much. So, instead of talking too much, and the other kinder and I are on different speaking-- you know, on speakers [INAUDIBLE] and so on for high schools, and for Hadassah, and so on. And we have decided, as we are teachers, we would write some of the testimonies which I have collected-- which are lying in the archives at Gratz College-- and write them up into a book form to be used for the young readers. Because we were-- the majority of us were 11. The youngest kinder we know is-- was under two-- was a babe in arms. And the oldest had to be 17 of the Kindertransport, because they would have otherwise be having to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we have written a book, the testimonies into story form, for the young people to read under the different categories, life before Hitler, life during Hitler, preparation, the Kristallnacht, the journey, England, and World War II, and then reunions.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was-- is the hardest, because of the 10,000 children who were saved by England between the Kristallnacht, between December '38 and the outbreak of war in September '39-- of the 10,000, more than a third became instant orphans, although they didn't know it. They heard after the war that they-- their parents had perished. And they were alone. This is something that is very hard. And it's very interesting in our groups, that we have, in our support groups, it was the hope of reunification that I think kept us alive. And struggle, and the hope, and we didn't want to let our parents down, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: That really kept us going. Except, I think, the survivors of the fittest also. But we probably also had good genes. Who knows what made us survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: But I realized since '89, and I guess it's because we are getting older also, the short-term memory isn't so good. I don't remember where I put my glasses or what I had for breakfast yesterday, but the early childhood memories come back. And the more that one talks and hears about it, the more triggers come up. And you realize what Hitler did to our lives. It really changed the whole of our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who the heck would have thought in Hamburg in 1927 that I would be going to England, to Israel, to Brazil, to Israel again many times, and to America? And to return to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then come, and then to-- yeah, well. That was an interesting thing. My father, when my mother died, wanted to see the graves. He was in his high 80s. And he asked me to go with him back to Hamburg. He only had one eye, and he was with-- was friendly with a lady, also from Hamburg, Katie, and Katie has a daughter in Miami. So the four of us went back to Hamburg. And they-- we went to the cemeteries, and made sure that there was perpetual care, and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they had a ball. Why? Because they had a good time. They had their youth. And that's where we went dancing, and asked, do you remember this? And, Katie, do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And those two old fogies, to us then, had a wonderful time. They wanted to see the museums, and they wanted to do-- go to have coffee here and tea there. And all I could see at the [NON-ENGLISH] house in the area of the Kristallnacht were Germans in uniforms. They didn't see that. They had already left. To me, it was a horrible vacation-- so-called vacation. I only saw the negative stuff. However, the older I'm getting now, in talking about my early years, they were happy. They were stable, to a great extent. They were loving, with relatives, and uncles, and aunts, and visitors. And that's basically--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a strange thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what kind of message would you like to leave to your grandchildren, to your children, for them? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is you have to fight in life to get what you really feel you need for your comfort level. You have to fight for it. To me, Judaism is a-- and being Jewish is an honor. It's something to fight for that is worth while. My message to my children really is there will always be antisemitism. There always has been, and there always will be. And you just have to accept that. Live a decent life. And basically, if you think of the five-- the Ten Commandments, all religions have the same basics. And if we can live them, live according to that, it would be a better world. But fight, and stand up, and be heard. Don't take-- don't be a bystander, as there were so many during the war, and keep your mouth closed. Do what is the decent, right thing to do, and stand up and fight for it. That's basically my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Eva. I know that we are in the home of a friend of yours--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: --whose history is a long one with you. And we're going to include her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, good. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be good.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but thank you very much for sharing your experience with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Did you put in a preface?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to ask you to introduce yourself, Ruth-- you have just come into this picture-- and to tell us what the relationship is between you, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm Ruth Chernoff. I was born [NON-ENGLISH] in Hamburg, Germany. And our families, Eva's and mine, have known each other for a long, long time. I don't know exactly when they met, but we grew up together. And I remember spending almost every other weekend going out on excursions, come rain or shine, Sunday, we went out on the subway to the end, one way or another, and went hiking, and climbing, and had picnics together. We had a very nice time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit just-- a little bit about your family background then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was born and raised in Hamburg. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1921. And my grandfather, my father's father, was the cantor at the new temple in Hamburg. He was pretty well known. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Mulightz ?] [? Hindle. ?] And he composed, did a lot of compositions that are even being used now in America and in some of the synagogues. And my, my mother's family came from Nuremberg and lived in Nuremberg. And we used to go there once a year for vacations to visit with my grandparents there. My grandfather, my maternal grandfather, was an importer of woolens and piece goods from England. And he had a big business there, which was later taken over by the Nazis. The whole building was confiscated. And my grandparents [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] stayed in, in Nuremberg until the end of 1939. I came over here with my dad and at the beginning of '39. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Over here, meaning to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the United States, yeah, directly from Hamburg. And my mother stayed and went to Nuremberg to get her her parents out. And her sister and husband were in Amsterdam. That's what she wanted to bring them. And interesting sideline there is the reason they ended up in Amsterdam is because my, my mother's brother-in-law was an opera singer in Weimar. And he had a French woman who was also an opera singer, who happened to be Hermann Goering's wife. And she was the one who saw to it that they got out of the country into Amsterdam, and got the rest of the family out also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hermann Goering's wife was the person--
